MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT , Alternatively, Motion For Adjudication
Off Calendar Jun-08-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES - ATTY. EDITH J. BENAY, TEL. NO. 1-415-241-7286 APPEARING FOR THE DEFENDANT;
GERI LYNN GREEN, TEL. NO. 1-415-982-2600, DEFENDANT APPEARING IN PRO PER.

RULING - A R G U E D; The Court adopted its tentative ruling. PLAINTIFF JULIEN SWANSON'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT, Alternatively For Summary Adjudication is DENIED and his alternative motion for summary adjudication is GRANTED as to the fourth and sixth causes of action and denied as to the first, second, third, and fifth causes of action alleged in the cross-complaint filed by Law Offices of Green & Green, LLP. As to the first and second causes of action for promissory fraud and intentional misrepresentation, Mr. Swanson satisfied his initial burden of showing that he lacked fraudulent intent when he made the promises and representations that are the bases for those claims. Mr. Swanson stated that each of his invoices and accountings was a good faith attempt to accurately reflect his time spent, the task performed, the invoice amounts, and the payments made. (Swanson Facts 97 and 98; Swanson Declaration137 and 138.) The LLP presented evidence creating a triable issue as to fraudulent intent due to Mr. Swanson's delay in invoicing, missing invoices, submission of invoicing around a time when partner Mark Green suffered from severe medical conditions, inaccuracies in invoice line items, and misstatements in accountings regarding whether Mr. Swanson submitted the companion invoices. (Green Declaration 38, 55-56 and 61; Responses to Swanson Facts 43, 56(1), 57(4), 57(7)). As to the third cause of action for intentional interference with contractual relations, Mr. Swanson did not maintain his initial burden. Mr. Swanson has not shown that he was unaware of a contract between Green & Green and the Lahey clients. As to the fifth cause of action for breach of written contract, Mr. Swanson did not maintain his initial burden. He failed to show that the parties did not orally extend the contract at the time it was executed. He also failed to explain how the written agreement was fully integrated thus precluding parol evidence of oral modification. As to the fourth and sixth causes of action for breach of oral contract and quantum meruit, Mr. Swanson maintains his initial burden by showing the parties did not enter into an oral agreement in April 2013 that the law firm would provide office space and services and that Mr. Swanson would pay the reasonable value of those services. (Swanson's Fact 118; Swanson's Exhibit KK.) The LLP did not present any evidence creating a triable issue on those two claims. The only evidence in opposition is Ms. Green's declaration that the parties never agreed Mr. Swanson would reduce his hourly charge from $75/hour to $50/hour for use of office space and staff. (Green Declaration 58.) The LLP failed to provide evidence of the existence of an oral contract or any basis entitling it to quantum meruit recovery. Prevailing party to prepare a form of order and to comply with CCP 437c(g) and CRC 3.1312. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter: Carol Karen, CSR #8189 =302/hk

Defendants Geri Green And Law Offices Of Green And Green Llp'S Motion For Summary Judgment Or, In The Alternate Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Jun-08-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: APPEARANCES - ATTY. EDITH J. BENAY, TEL. NO. 1-415-241-7286 APPEARING FOR THE DEFENDANT;
GERI LYNN GREEN, TEL. NO. 1-415-982-2600, DEFENDANT APPEARING IN PRO PER.

RULING - A R G U E D; The Court adopted its tentative ruling. Defendant Geri Geen and the Law offices of Green & Green, LLP Motion for Summary Judgment or in the alternate Summary Adjudication - Defendant Geri Green's motion for summary judgment and alternative motion for summary adjudication is off calendar. Defendant Law Offices of Green and Green, LLP's motion for summary judgment and alternative motion for summary adjudication of the first, second, and fourth causes of action in plaintiff Julien Swanson's first amended complaint is denied in its entirety. The portion of the motion brought by Ms. Green is moot since she was dismissed on May 9, 2017. The LLP's motion is not untimely. The motion was served on Mr. Swanson on March 22, 2017 and, to the extent that Mr. Swanson received insufficient notice of the motion, he waived any objection on that ground by opposing the motion on the merits. The LLP failed to satisfy its initial burden of showing that all claims in the FAC are barred by the doctrine of unclean hands. Even if the LLP had satisfied its burden, Mr. Swanson has shown that there are multiple triable issues of fact precluding summary judgment on the defense of unclean hands. LLP's proffered facts do not show that Mr. Swanson inflated his billings or that he committed other inequitable conduct that warrants denying him all of the relief he seeks. (See LLP Facts 9-11 and 16-19). Moreover, Mr. Swanson presented evidence creating triable issues whether his billings were accurate and whether he engaged in any billing misconduct. (E.g. Responses to LLP Facts 14, 20, 23, 26-27, 31-32, 46, and 49-51). As to the first cause of action for breach of the Roberts contract, the LLP did not maintain its initial burden because it failed to include any fact in its separate statement that the written Roberts retainer agreement precluded oral modification. As to the second cause of action for breach of the Lahey contract, the LLP did not maintain its initial burden because it did not submit any evidence showing the existence of a surety agreement between Mr. Green and Mr. Swanson or that Mr. Green benefitted personally from the arrangement. As to the fourth cause of action for breach of contract on the remaining cases, even assuming the LLP satisfied its initial burden, there is a triable issue whether Mr. Swanson was paid all of the amounts he was owed pursuant to the oral agreements for the work he did in the remaining cases. For example, Mr. Swanson disputes the $2,500 payment made on December 4, 2013 and states the amount should not have been applied to outstanding invoices. (Response to LLP Fact 108). Mr. Swanson also disputes the $5,000 payment made in February/March 2014 (Response to LLP Fact 20) and the $5102.19 payment made on May 1, 2014 (Response to LLP Fact 23). Prevailing party to prepare a form of order and to comply with CCP 437c(g) and CRC 3.1312. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter: Carol Karen, CSR #8189 =302/hk

The Court confirmed its tentative ruling. DEFENDANTS GERI GREEN, LAW OFFICES OF GREEN AND GREEN LLP and LAW OFFICES OF GERI LYNN GREEN LC'S DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT is sustained with twenty days leave to amend as to all three claims in the complaint alleged against Ms. Green and the first two causes of action alleged against the law firm and overruled as to the third cause of action alleged against the law firm. The second claim for breach of the implied covenant does not adequately allege breach of an implied term separate from breach of the express term by failing to pay plaintiff Julien Swanson what he is allegedly owed under the agreement or agreements that were allegedly breached by defendants. The third claim for quantum meruit is adequately alleged against the law firm. Order signed.
